learning_outcomes.description: Understand the concepts of power and structure, and agency using a range of theoretical perspectives.
Understand the concepts of human rights, social justice, and diversity, using relevant theoretical perspectives.
Articulate challenges facing people in contemporary Australia in terms of human rights, social justice, and respect for diversity.
Apply the concepts of power, structure and agency to explain prevailing challenges around social justice, human rights, and respect for diversity in contemporary Australia.
Identify how social workers can take effective responsibility for human rights, social justice, and respect for diversity in contemporary Australia.

assessments.task_description:
The three assessment items in this course have been designed to build on each other in the form of a project:

  • Assessment 1 consists of a quiz that tests your knowledge and understanding of key concepts of power, structure, agency, human rights, social justice, and respect for diversity, which you will use in preparing for Assessment 2. It is scheduled for Week 4 and comprises 20% of your course mark.
  • Assessment 2 consists of a set of group-based activities at the end of which you will submit a portfolio containing a peer-reviewed conference abstract on a human rights, social justice or diversity-related concern of your choice. The portfolio is due in Week 8 and comprises 30% of your course mark.
  • Assessment 3 entails the completion of an in-person conference presentation with PowerPoint slides, based on the abstract produced for Assessment 2. In addition to the concepts tested in Assessment 1, this task also requires you to draw on concepts of agency, responsibility, and allyship for human rights, social justice and respect for diversity. Your PowerPoint slides are due at the start of Week 13, with in-person presentations scheduled throughout that week. All presentations will be recorded and, along with your PowerPoint slides, comprise 50% of your course mark.

You will be producing the conference abstract (Assessment 2) and the conference presentation (Assessment 3) with reference to a fictitious student conference titled Advancing Human Rights, Social Justice, and Respect for Diversity in Australia Today. At the same time, your course coordinator is part of an international group of social work lecturers working to organise an actual symposium where students from selected universities can share their concerns around human rights, social justice, and diversity and discuss ideas about how these might be addressed. The envisaged symposium will be held in conjunction with World Social Work Day in March 2027. Interested students from this class will have an opportunity to participate in this event, and updates will be provided throughout the semester.

Your first assessment task is an in-class/in-person quiz. This initial task serves to assess your knowledge of the course material you studied during Weeks 1 to 3 of this course, that is (1) the concepts of power, structure, and agency, and (2) the concepts of human rights, social justice, and diversity. 

Your quiz is designed as a closed-book assessment and will contain 40 multiple-choice questions, each worth 0.5 marks. The quiz is scheduled for Wednesday, 19 August 2026 at 14h00, and must be completed within 40 minutes (plus 10 minutes reading time). As this quiz is taken in class/in person, you must attend the lecture period during which it is scheduled to complete this task. 

Note: This assessment task must be completed in person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of Generative AI may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
This description is only a summary of the task. For full details, including the marking rubric, please refer to the course Learn UQ (Blackboard) site.

During Week 4, students will be allocated to groups of three to work collaboratively on their second assessment task, a peer-reviewed conference abstract.

Each student will produce an abstract of maximally 300 words to provide the outline of arguments for a ten-minute-long paper to be presented at a (fictitious) social work student conference entitled Advancing Human Rights, Social Justice, and Respect for Diversity in Australia Today. In your abstract, you will delineate how your proposed paper will explain the concepts of power and structure, and how these concepts will illuminate a key concern that you, as a citizen and future professional, have regarding human rights, social justice, and diversity.

Each group member will review and critique their group members’ respective abstracts. Thereafter, they will respond to feedback received from their group members on their draft abstract and revise them accordingly. This work will be completed both during tutorial times and at home. At the end of Week 8, all students will submit individual portfolios containing their initial draft abstracts, a table containing the feedback and their responses to the feedback received, and their final revised abstract, for assessment. To complete this task, tutorial attendance during Weeks 5 to 8 is compulsory.   

Note: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of this assessment will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment requires students to submit an electronic version of their assignment via Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

26/10/2026 12:00 pm

PowerPoint slides are due in Week 13 on Monday, 26/10/2026, 12:00 pm

Presentations are scheduled for Week 13, from Tuesday, 27/10/2026, to Thursday, 29/10/2026.

All presentations are conducted in person and will be scheduled outside of regular tutorial times.
assessments.task_description:
This description is only a summary of the task. For full details, including the marking rubric, please refer to the course Learn UQ (Blackboard) site.

In Week 13, you will complete your third assessment task, a recorded 10-minute PowerPoint presentation to a (fictitious) social work student conference entitled Advancing Human Rights, Social Justice, and Respect for Diversity in Australia Today. Your presentation will be based on the abstract submitted in Week 8, taking into consideration the marker's feedback. In your presentation, you will explain the concepts of power, structure, and agency. You will explain, further, how these concepts help to (1) illuminate a key concern that you, as a citizen and future professional, have regarding human rights, social justice, and respect for diversity in Australia today, as well as (2) inform what you, as a citizen and future professional, believe you can do about it.

Your task entails two parts: (1) to prepare and submit a set of PowerPoint slides on your chosen topic, followed by (2) a 10-minute in-person presentation to a group of peers, supported by your slides. The presentation will be recorded. Both your slides and the presentation will be assessed.

Note: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of this assessment will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment requires students to submit an electronic version of their assignment via Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_activities.topic:
Course Introduction and Overview of Expectations

This introductory lecture provides an overview of the course content, delivery mode, assessment tasks, and performance expectations.

In the second half of the lecture, we commence Part 1 of this course by introducing the concepts of power, structure and agency, and discussing our initial ideas about human rights, social justice, and diversity..
learning_activities.topic_description: Course Introduction and Overview of Expectations This introductory lecture provides an overview of the course content, delivery mode, assessment tasks, and performance expectations. In the second half of the lecture, we commence Part 1 of this course by introducing the concepts of power, structure and agency, and discussing our initial ideas about human rights , social justice , and diversity ..

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction to the Concepts of Power, Structure, and Agency

In this lecture, we introduce the different theoretical perspectives from which power, structure and agency can be understood and discuss some of the practical implications of these differences.

Power, Structure, and Agency (Assessment 1 Preparation)

We will conduct activities to deepen your understanding of the content covered in your Week 2 lecture, address any open questions, and discuss the type of questions you will be asked in your first assessment task.

Human Rights, Social Justice, and Respect for Diversity

In Week 3, we explore how human rights, social justice, and respect for diversity are understood in social work and consider some of the key debates surrounding these terms.

learning_activities.topic:
Assessment 1 and Setting up of Assessment 2 Groups

Week 4 marks the end of Part 1 and the beginning of Part 2 of this course:

In our Week 4 lecture period, we will set up the groups in which you will work during Weeks 5 to 8 to complete your second assessment task. This is also your final opportunity to ask any remaining questions about your first assessment task (in-class quiz).

The quiz is scheduled for Wednesday, 19.08.26 (please consult your timetable for details), and will test your understanding of the concepts covered in Weeks 1 to 3 of this course. As the quiz will be conducted under exam conditions, attendance is a requirement, and an attendance register will be kept.

This is also your final opportunity to ask any remaining questions about your first assessment task ( in-class quiz ). The quiz is scheduled for Wednesday, 19.08.26 (please consult your timetable for details), and will test your understanding of the concepts covered in Weeks 1 to 3 of this course. As the quiz will be conducted under exam conditions, attendance is a requirement , and an attendance register will be kept.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction to Assessment 2

In Part 2 of this course, we explore the concepts taught in Part 1 in relation to the student’s topics of choice. We begin by reviewing the task description and marking criteria of the second assessment (a portfolio containing a peer-reviewed conference abstract) and discussing how this task builds on the work covered in Assessment 1.
learning_activities.topic_description: Introduction to Assessment 2 In Part 2 of this course, we explore the concepts taught in Part 1 in relation to the student’s topics of choice. We begin by reviewing the task description and marking criteria of the second assessment ( a portfolio containing a peer-reviewed conference abstract ) and discussing how this task builds on the work covered in Assessment 1.

learning_activities.topic:
A Post-structural Perspective on Power, Structure, and Agency and the Challenges Facing People in Australia Today

In this lecture, we examine how the concepts of power, structure, and agency may be applied to explain some of the challenges experienced in contemporary Australia regarding human rights, social justice, and diversity, using a critical post-structuralist lens.
learning_activities.topic_title: A Post-structural Perspective on Power, Structure, and Agency and the Challenges Facing People in Australia Today
learning_activities.topic_description: A Post-structural Perspective on Power, Structure, and Agency and the Challenges Facing People in Australia Today In this lecture, we examine how the concepts of power, structure, and agency may be applied to explain some of the challenges experienced in contemporary Australia regarding human rights, social justice, and diversity, using a critical post-structuralist lens.

learning_activities.topic:
Assessment 2 Session One

In Week 5, students start working on the first draft of their own conference abstracts. As this tutorial forms part of the second assessment task, attendance is a requirement, and an attendance register is taken.

learning_activities.topic:
Feminist and Anti-oppressive Perspectives on Power, Structure, and Agency, and the Challenges Facing People in Australia Today

In our Week 6 lecture, we examine how the concepts of power, structure, and agency may be applied to explain some of the challenges experienced in contemporary Australia regarding human rights, social justice, and diversity, using a feminist and anti-oppressive lens.

Assessment 2 Session Two

During this session, students start providing feedback on each other’s abstracts. This tutorial forms part of the second assessment task, and an attendance register is taken.

learning_activities.topic:
Critical Race Theory and a Decolonial Perspective on Power, Structure, and Agency, and the Challenges Facing People in Australia Today

In Week 7, we examine how the concepts of power, structure and agency may be applied to explain some of the challenges experienced in contemporary Australia regarding human rights, social justice, and diversity, using the lenses of critical race and decolonial theories.

learning_activities.topic:
Assessment 2 Session Three

During the Week 7 tutorial session, students have the opportunity to discuss mutual feedback on each other's abstracts and consider how to address it. Attendance at this tutorial remains required for assessment purposes, and an attendance register is kept.

learning_activities.topic:
Micro-perspectives on Power, Structure, and Agency and the Challenges Facing People in Australia Today

In Week 8, we examine a selection of micro-perspectives on power, structure, and agency and consider their relevance in explaining some of the challenges experienced in contemporary Australia regarding human rights, social justice, and diversity.

learning_activities.topic:
Assessment 2 Session Four

Based on the feedback provided, students revise their drafts and produce a second, final version of their conference abstracts. As portfolios are due at the end of Week 8, these abstracts should be completed or near completion by the end of the Week 8 tutorial. This is the final session, during which attendance is required for assessment purposes, and once more, an attendance register is kept.

The submission of a portfolio containing a peer-reviewed conference abstract marks the end of Part 2 of this course.

The submission of a portfolio containing a peer-reviewed conference abstract marks the end of Part 2 of this course.

learning_activities.topic:
Multiple Perspectives on What to Do About the Challenges Facing People in Australia Today

Week 9 marks the beginning of Part 3 of this course. 

As our focus shifts to the question of how to address some of the challenges experienced in contemporary Australia around issues of human rights, social justice, and respect for diversity, we commence by re-examining the types of action to which the different lenses applied during the preceding four weeks give rise.

What to Do About the Challenges Facing People in Australia Today: Introduction to Assessment 3

We revise our Week 9 lecture content. And, after introducing your final assessment piece (conference presentation with PowerPoint slides), we discuss how this task builds on the work completed for Assessments 1 and 2.

What to Do about Human Rights, Social Justice and Respect for Diveresity in Australia Today: Assessment 3 Preparation

We continue our preparations for Assessment 3 (conference presentation and PowerPoint slides) by reviewing your Assessment 2 feedback and considering (1) key learnings for the preparation of your PowerPoint slides and conference presentation, and (2) which additional content you still need to include.

learning_activities.topic:
Taking Responsibility

In our Week 10 lecture, we consider the concept of responsibility and discuss how it is connected to those of agency, structure, and power. We also explore its potential to contribute to advancing human rights, social justice, and respect for diversity in Australia today.
learning_activities.topic_description: Taking Responsibility In our Week 10 lecture, we consider the concept of responsibility and discuss how it is connected to those of agency, structure, and power. We also explore its potential to contribute to advancing human rights, social justice, and respect for diversity in Australia today.

learning_activities.topic:
Taking Responsibility: Assessment 3 Preparation

We revise our Week 11 lecture content and provide an opportunity to ask questions about the third assessment task.
learning_activities.topic_description: Taking Responsibility: Assessment 3 Preparation We revise our Week 11 lecture content and provide an opportunity to ask questions about the third assessment task.

learning_activities.topic:
Becoming an Ally

We explore the concept of allyship and discuss how it is connected to those of agency, structure, and power. We ask: What is its potential to contribute to advancing human rights, social justice, and respect for diversity in contemporary Australia?

This is our final lecture before you have the opportunity to present your semester's work.

Becoming an Ally: Assessment 3 Preparation

We revise the Week 12 lecture content, as well as give students an opportunity to discuss any challenges they may be experiencing in preparing the third assessment task. We jointly consider solutions.

Course Summary and Revision

In this final lecture, we will revisit the key concepts and ideas covered across the semester. This will also be an opportunity to provide feedback on the course, which will inform its future deliveries.
